terms.definition: A monetary regime in which each unit of currency is legally redeemable for a fixed quantity of gold, constraining money creation.
A sustained general increase in the price level of goods and services, resulting in a decline in purchasing power.
The degree to which the financial system’s abstract claims map onto real productive capacity and resources.
A metric equal to total public debt divided by annual gross domestic product, indicating leverage at the sovereign level.
Central-bank policy of expanding the monetary base to support higher nominal spending or government deficits.
Nominal financial claims not backed by equivalent productive capacity and sustainable only through rolling credit.
A financial contract whose value is based on an underlying asset, interest rate, or index.
Large-scale asset purchases by a central bank that expand its balance sheet and increase the monetary base.
A regime shift where traditional flow from work to wealth reverses, making credit creation and asset inflation primary drivers.
Sustained increases in the prices of financial and real assets, often decoupled from underlying productive value.
The use of borrowed funds to amplify potential returns (and losses) on an investment.
Alignment between culturally endorsed virtues and the material incentives embedded in the economic system.
The capacity of major social institutions to transmit accurate information and enforce honest norms.
